CSCE 222 Syllabus

Section 500 (46464)

Discrete Structures for Computing

Spring 2026 - College Station

Course Information

Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC

Meeting Days: TR

Start Time: 08:00AM

End Time: 09:15AM

Start Date: 01/12/2026

End Date: 05/05/2026

Meeting Location:  HRBB 124

Credit Hours:  3

Instructor Details

Dylan Shell

Email:  dshell@tamu.edu

Office Location:  PETR 315

Phone:  (979) 845-2369

Office Hours

Walk-ins: Wednesdays, 3:00pm–4:00pm (otherwise by appointment)

Catalog Description

Discrete Structures for Computing. (3-0). Credit 3. Mathematical foundations from

discrete mathematics for analyzing computer algorithms, for both correctness and

performance; introduction to models of computation, including finite state

machines and Turing machines. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in MATH 142,

MATH 147, MATH 151, or MATH 171. Cross-listed with ECEN 222.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in MATH 142, MATH 147,

MATH 151, or MATH 171. Cross Listing: ECEN 222/CSCE 222.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the student who has done the requisite work will

be able to:

understand the basic principles of logic, proofs and sets.

apply results from discrete mathematics to the analysis of algorithms.

produce proofs by induction and apply counting techniques.

explain models of computation.

Special Course Designation

None

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

The due dates for the homework will always be at least 7 days after the

homework is posted, and will be designated via canvas. Occasionally the

instructor will permit a moderate extension to the deadlines, those extensions

will apply to every student equally, and will be communicated in class and by

updating the details in the canvas submission system.

The course has a late policy with a discount of 33⅓ % per day. This is

computed by the minute (not as a step-function at midnight) which works out

to follow this rule (where  t  is in units of minutes late):

AwardedScore  = max(0,(4320 -  t )/4320) ⋅   RawScoreAsIfNotLate

The discounting is linear and reaches zero after 3 days.

Proof: 60 minutes in an hour, 24 hours in a day, so 3 days means

60×24×3 = 4320. Of course 33⅓ ×3 = 1.

The midterm and final exams, being examinations, are not permitted to be

late; they will be in-person written examinations. Midterms will be taken

during class.

Standard university reasons for lateness shall, of course, be respected so long

as the student communicates with the instructor as soon as possible—this

means, specifically, it has been communicated before the deadline has passed

and not  ex post facto  whenever feasible. (Note that job interviews and

attendance of the career fair are, as per Student Rule 7, generally not excused

absences: but the instructor will judge them in his own discretion.)

Week-by-week topic breakdown

Comprehensive Final Exam (Thursday, May 1st, 1:00-3:00 PM)

Homework assignments

The only submissions for this course will be homeworks, these are to be completed

in LaTeX and submitted electronically via canvas. There will be approximately six

of these posted, thus, at a rate of about one every two weeks. They involve a

substantial effort, despite only counting for 15% of the grade. They are intended as

the exercises used to ensure you understand the material and prepare for the

examinations. We do have a pair of undergraduate TAs who will help with the

grading, however they will check only a subset of the questions for correctness, and

assess the submission for completeness. Solutions will be posted ±3 days after the

deadline.

AI Statement

Use of online resources, AI-tools, friends, etc. for your

homework assignments

The homeworks are intended to give you cause to dig into the textbook, to think

about the material (including pondering weird edge-cases) and gain facility with

doing the mathematics. You may find other resources (e.g., online forums, your

friends and classmates, your TAs, AI-tools) helpful. Any of these may be used, so

long as you acknowledge this fact in your submissions. Attempt the homework, but

use other materials once you get stuck: the more stubbornly you try things yourself,

the better for development of your own understanding (within reason, because you

are using time that displaces other learning experiences). The portion of the grade

for the homeworks is really to provide enough incentive to do the homework

assignments without them being "starved" by the other demands of your life.
